pattern: Two strings are isomorphic when their character-position patterns are identical; verify this by maintaining a bidirectional mapping between characters of s and t, checking consistency at each position.
hints: Walk both strings in lockstep; at each index, the character in s must always map to the same character in t and vice versa — any inconsistency means they are not isomorphic.
Keep two dictionaries: one mapping each character of s to the corresponding character of t, and a reverse mapping from t back to s. If either mapping conflicts at any position, return False.
A cleaner alternative: transform each string into its 'pattern' by replacing every character with the index of its first occurrence, then compare the two resulting patterns.

edge_inputs.description: both strings are single identical characters — trivially isomorphic
single characters that are different — still isomorphic (one-to-one mapping)
all characters the same in both — isomorphic
s has repeated pattern but t does not — not isomorphic
two different s-chars cannot map to the same t-char (injective requirement)
forward mapping is consistent but backward mapping fails — e.g. two s-chars mapping to one t-char
longer string where inconsistency appears only late in the scan

stress.description: random isomorphic string pairs via shared pattern, length up to 50000
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = max(1, size)
    # Build a random pattern index sequence
    # to guarantee the pair is isomorphic
    chars_s = list(string.ascii_lowercase)
    chars_t = list(string.ascii_uppercase)
    rng.shuffle(chars_s)
    rng.shuffle(chars_t)
    # Map: each unique symbol in a template maps to one char in s and one in t
    num_unique = rng.randint(1, min(26, n))
    pattern = [rng.randint(0, num_unique - 1) for _ in range(n)]
    s = ''.join(chars_s[p] for p in pattern)
    t = ''.join(chars_t[p] for p in pattern)
    return [s, t]
stress.note: pairs are always isomorphic by construction
